title: Dog Ingested Paint Thinner: Immediate Emergency Steps & Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog ingested paint thinner, this is a high-level emergency. Learn why immediate vet care is vital and what information you need to save them.
content_html:
TL;DR: Ingesting paint thinner is a life-threatening emergency for dogs that requires immediate veterinary intervention. Do not induce vomiting at home, as this can cause fatal respiratory damage and severe chemical burns.

What is paint thinner and why is it toxic to dogs?

Paint thinners are potent chemical solvents, typically composed of hydrocarbons like mineral spirits, turpentine, or acetone. These substances are highly corrosive and toxic to dogs, affecting the gastrointestinal tract, central nervous system, and respiratory system. Because these liquids are volatile, they pose a severe risk if they are swallowed or inhaled.

Is paint thinner ingestion a veterinary emergency for my dog?

  • Urgency Level: High. Ingesting paint thinner is a life-threatening emergency.
  • These chemicals cause severe chemical burns to the mouth and esophagus, and if aspirated into the lungs, they can cause fatal pneumonia.
  • Symptoms may include extreme lethargy, seizures, vomiting, or difficulty breathing.
  • Contact an emergency veterinarian or a pet poison control center immediately.
  • Do not attempt to induce vomiting at home, as the chemical can cause even more damage to the throat and lungs on the way back up.

How does a photo of the product label help the veterinarian?

  • Quickly snap a clear photo of the product label and the ingredient list before heading to the vet.
  • Knowing the exact ingredients allows the veterinary team to provide a more targeted and effective treatment plan.
  • A photo of the area where the ingestion occurred can help the vet estimate the volume consumed, which is critical for determining the level of toxicity.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of paint thinner ingestion in dogs, emetics and gastric lavage are generally not recommended due to the increased risk of aspiration. Instead, activated charcoal and saline cathartics may be considered to reduce absorption. Supportive therapy is crucial to address potential complications such as shock, liver and kidney damage, respiratory failure, and acidosis.

Chapter: Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3046)

faqs.question: Should I give my dog milk or water after ingestion?
faqs.answer: Do not give your dog anything by mouth unless specifically instructed by a veterinarian. Certain liquids can actually speed up the absorption of chemicals or increase the risk of vomiting and aspiration.

faqs.question: What if my dog only got paint thinner on their fur?
faqs.answer: This is still an emergency because dogs will naturally try to lick the chemical off, leading to ingestion. You should prevent them from licking and call a vet immediately for instructions on how to safely decontaminate their skin.

faqs.question: What are the common signs of hydrocarbon poisoning?
faqs.answer: Common signs include a strong chemical odor on the breath, excessive drooling, pawing at the mouth, gagging, unsteadiness, and labored breathing.
